name: Oedipus
description: Oedipus, while not technically a deity in the classical Greek pantheon, occupies a liminal space within Greek mythology, embodying themes of fate, free will, and the tragic consequences of hubris. He is primarily known as the King of Thebes, whose life was predetermined by a prophecy foretelling that he would kill his father and marry his mother. Despite attempts to circumvent this fate by both his parents and himself, the prophecy tragically comes to fruition. Oedipus's story serves as a profound exploration of human agency in the face of seemingly insurmountable divine decree, and the inherent limitations of mortal knowledge. His self-inflicted blindness after discovering the truth becomes a powerful symbol of insight gained too late, and the devastating power of knowledge. The enduring relevance of Oedipus lies in his representation of the human condition, grappling with questions of destiny, morality, and the burden of self-awareness. He is a figure of both immense suffering and profound understanding, leaving a lasting impact on Western literature and thought.
subtitle: The Fated King

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy and Abandonment
keyMyths.description: Before Oedipus's birth, an oracle foretold that Laius, King of Thebes, would be killed by his own son. Fearing this prophecy, Laius and Jocasta abandoned their infant son, Oedipus, on Mount Cithaeron, piercing his ankles and binding them together. This act, intended to avert fate, ironically sets in motion the chain of events that lead to its fulfillment. The abandonment highlights the futility of attempting to escape one's destiny, and the power of the divine will in shaping mortal lives.

keyMyths.title: Killing Laius and Solving the Sphinx's Riddle
keyMyths.description: While traveling, Oedipus encountered Laius at a crossroads and, in a fit of anger fueled by a dispute over right of way, unknowingly killed him, thus fulfilling the first part of the prophecy. He then arrived in Thebes, plagued by the Sphinx, a monstrous creature who posed riddles to travelers, devouring those who failed to answer correctly. Oedipus correctly answered the Sphinx's riddle, freeing Thebes and earning the throne, as well as the hand of the widowed Queen Jocasta.

keyMyths.title: The Discovery and Self-Blinding
keyMyths.description: A plague struck Thebes, and the oracle revealed that it would only cease when Laius's murderer was brought to justice. Oedipus, determined to save his city, launched an investigation, uncovering the horrifying truth: he had killed his father and married his mother. Jocasta, upon realizing the truth, hanged herself, and Oedipus, in a fit of despair and self-punishment, blinded himself with Jocasta's brooches, accepting the full weight of his tragic fate and the consequences of his actions.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: The foundational tragedy depicting Oedipus's downfall and self-discovery.
